Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic

Glossary About
Minds and mechanisms · 17th – 18th c.

Early Modern

The philosophy of mind meets the scientific revolution: new metaphysics, new psychologies, and new experiments in materialism, idealism, and religious introspection. Rationalists, empiricists, and their critics all try to say what a thinking being is once the older vocabulary of soul no longer goes unquestioned.

1588 – 1679

Thomas Hobbes

A thoroughgoing materialist: thought as motion in the body, the mind as mechanism.

1596 – 1650

René Descartes

The thinking thing, the body as extension, and a layered metaphysics of human, divine, and angelic minds.

1632 – 1677

Baruch Spinoza

Mind and body as two attributes of one substance: parallelism, conatus, and the intellectual love of God.

1632 – 1704

John Locke

Ideas, consciousness, and the invention of personal identity as a forensic concept.

1646 – 1716

Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz

Monads, petites perceptions, and a psychology built entirely from within.

1710 – 1796

Thomas Reid

Scottish common sense: perception, belief, and the rejection of the theory of ideas.

1711 – 1776

David Hume

A bundle of perceptions: the self as habit, and the limits of introspection.

1777 – 1847

Mary Shepherd

A rigorous realist reply to Berkeley and Hume: causation, mind, and the external world.

1724 – 1804

Immanuel Kant

The transcendental turn: apperception, the categories, and the self as the I-think of experience.

1685 – 1753

George Berkeley

Berkeley’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as an active-spirit theory.

1618 – 1680

Elisabeth of Bohemia

I will treat this as a report on Elisabeth’s own philosophy of mind, not merely as a footnote to Descartes.

1692 – 1752

Joseph Butler

Memory, habit, and the evolutionary continuity of mind and life.

1623 – 1673

Margaret Cavendish

Self-moving matter and a fully embodied philosophy of mind.

1706 – 1749

Émilie du Châtelet

A comprehensive philosophy of mind can be extracted from Du Châtelet, but it must be reconstructed rather than simply quoted from a…

1631 – 1679

Anne Conway

Spirit, vitality, and a living continuum between mind and nature.

1713 – 1784

Denis Diderot

The most coherent reconstruction of Diderot’s philosophy of mind is a vital-materialist, embodied, developmental monism.

1709 – 1751

Julien Offray de La Mettrie

Materialism, embodiment, and the machine that feels and thinks.

1659 – 1708

Damaris Cudworth Masham

Moral agency, education, and the reflective life beyond Lockean orthodoxy.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
